sentences.base_language: Tonight I'm making a quiche for dinner.
sentences.target_language: Ce soir, je fais une quiche pour le dîner.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ce soir** = tonight/this evening — **ce** + time word: ce soir, ce matin, cet après-midi (cet before vowel).
**je fais** = I'm making — **faire** (irregular): je fais, tu fais, il fait, nous faisons, vous faites, ils font.
French uses the present tense for actions happening tonight — there's no continuous '-ing' tense in French.

sentences.base_language: First, I preheat the oven to 180 degrees.
sentences.target_language: D'abord, je préchauffe le four à cent quatre-vingts degrés.
sentences.grammarNote:
**D'abord** = first/firstly — a common sequencing word. Others: **ensuite** (then/next), **après** (after), **enfin** (finally).
**cent quatre-vingts** — note the -s on **vingt** in **quatre-vingts** (80) when it ends the number. But: **quatre-vingt-dix** (90) — no -s when followed by more digits.
**le four à** = the oven at — **à** for temperature setting: à 180 degrés.

sentences.base_language: I mix the eggs, cream, and cheese in a bowl.
sentences.target_language: Je mélange les œufs, la crème et le fromage dans un bol.
sentences.grammarNote:
**les œufs** = the eggs — note the ligature **œ** and the plural: **un œuf** [œf] → **les œufs** [le zø] — the -f is silent in the plural, and the **z** sound comes from liaison.
**mélanger** = to mix (regular -er verb with spelling change: nous mélangeons — e added before -ons to keep the soft g sound).
**dans un bol** = in a bowl — **dans** for containers/enclosed spaces.

sentences.base_language: I add salt, pepper, and a pinch of nutmeg.
sentences.target_language: J'ajoute du sel, du poivre et une pincée de noix de muscade.
sentences.grammarNote:
**du sel, du poivre** — partitive articles (**de + le**) for uncountable ingredients: du sel, du beurre, de la farine.
**une pincée de** = a pinch of — a cooking measure; compare: une cuillère de (a spoonful of), une noix de (a knob of, e.g. butter).
**la noix de muscade** = nutmeg (literally 'the walnut of musk').

sentences.base_language: I pour the mixture into the pie crust.
sentences.target_language: Je verse le mélange dans la pâte brisée.
sentences.grammarNote:
**verser** = to pour (regular -er verb): je verse, tu verses, il verse.
**le mélange** = the mixture — from **mélanger** (to mix); the suffix **-age** forms a masculine noun from a verb: mélanger → le mélange, mélanger → le ménage.
**la pâte brisée** = shortcrust pastry (literally 'broken dough') — a classic French pastry base for quiche and tarts.

sentences.base_language: I put it in the oven for thirty minutes.
sentences.target_language: Je mets ça au four pendant trente minutes.
sentences.grammarNote:
**je mets** = I put — **mettre** is irregular: je mets, tu mets, il met, nous mettons, vous mettez, ils mettent.
**au four** = in/to the oven — **à + le = au**.
**pendant trente minutes** = for thirty minutes — **pendant** is used for completed/fixed durations with the present or passé composé: pendant + time. Compare **depuis** (ongoing duration).

sentences.base_language: While I wait, I prepare a green salad.
sentences.target_language: Pendant que j'attends, je prépare une salade verte.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Pendant que** = while — introduces a simultaneous action: pendant que j'attends (while I wait), pendant qu'il dort (while he sleeps).
**j'attends** = I wait — **attendre** (to wait) is a regular -re verb: j'attends, tu attends, il attend.
**une salade verte** = a green salad — adjective **verte** (feminine of **vert**) follows the noun.

**Ça sent bon** = it smells good — **sentir** + **bon** (adverb here, not adjective).
Note: no article before **bon** in this construction.
**ça** = that/it (informal subject pronoun, very common in spoken French instead of **cela**).
Variations: ça sent très bon (it smells really good) · ça ne sent pas bon (it doesn't smell good).

sentences.base_language: The quiche is ready. Let's eat!
sentences.target_language: La quiche est prête. On mange !
sentences.grammarNote:
**prête** = ready (feminine, agreeing with **la quiche**); masculine: **prêt**.
**On mange !** = let's eat! — using **on** in an exclamation is the natural French way to say 'let's' + verb: on y va ! (let's go!), on part ! (let's leave!).
Alternative: **Allons manger !** (let's go eat — using the **nous** imperative) — grammatically correct but more formal.
